---
title: "BASC vs the standard synthetic-control toolkit"
subtitle: "In-sample fit on the Abadie et al. (2015) West Germany reunification study"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 5.2
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
import os, warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")
import numpy as np, pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def _find_up(name, start, hops=6):
    d = os.path.abspath(start)
    for _ in range(hops):
        if os.path.exists(os.path.join(d, name)):
            return d
        d = os.path.dirname(d)
    return os.path.abspath(start)

ROOT = _find_up("basedata", os.getcwd())
BASE = os.path.join(ROOT, "basedata")
DATA = os.getcwd() if os.path.exists(os.path.join(os.getcwd(), "data", "basc_weights.csv")) \
    else os.path.join(ROOT, "review", "basc_westgermany")
DATA = os.path.join(DATA, "data")
```

## The question

Bayesian Adaptive Synthetic Control (BASC; *Bayesian Donor Set Selection in
Synthetic Controls*) is presented as a competitive-or-superior synthetic-control
estimator. Because the credibility of any synthetic control rests on how well it
reproduces the treated unit *before* the intervention, a minimum bar for such a
claim is a pre-treatment fit comparable to the standard toolkit.

This note compares BASC's in-sample (pre-1990) fit on West Germany against
`mlsynth`'s own estimators on the identical data (the Abadie, Diamond &
Hainmueller 2015 `repgermany` panel, original per-capita GDP scale), assembled
with `mlsynth`'s `compare_estimators` utility. The `mlsynth` analyses run at
render time; BASC is precomputed (its Gibbs sampler runs from the authors' R,
`github.com/sll-lee/paper-BASC`).

## Specifications

Standard SCM is run two ways. Outcome-only matches the pre-1990 GDP path. The
covariate specification is the canonical Abadie et al. (2015) predictor set:

- GDP per capita, inflation rate, trade openness, industry share — averaged
  **1981–1990**;
- investment rate and schooling — averaged **1980–1985**.

fit via two bilevel solvers: `backend="malo"` (the Malo et al. 2024 global
corner optimum) and `backend="mscmt"` (MSCMT's nested differential-evolution
search). The robust family is `CLUSTERSC` (RPCA-SC and PCR/RSC).

```{python}
#| label: compare
#| echo: true
#| code-fold: false
from mlsynth import VanillaSC, CLUSTERSC, compare_estimators

# --- ADH 2015 West Germany covariate specification ---
COVS = ["gdp_avg", "infrate", "trade", "industry", "schooling", "invest80"]
WINDOWS = {"gdp_avg": (1981, 1990), "infrate": (1981, 1990), "trade": (1981, 1990),
           "industry": (1981, 1990), "schooling": (1980, 1985), "invest80": (1980, 1985)}
FIT_WINDOW = (1960, 1989)          # outcome-matching (pre-treatment) window

raw = pd.read_stata(os.path.join(BASE, "repgermany.dta"))
d = raw[["country", "year", "gdp", "infrate", "trade", "industry", "schooling", "invest80"]].copy()
d["gdp_avg"] = d["gdp"]             # GDP per capita as an averaged predictor
d["treat"] = ((d.country == "West Germany") & (d.year >= 1990)).astype(int)

base = dict(df=d, outcome="gdp", treat="treat", unitid="country", time="year",
            display_graphs=False)
cov = dict(covariates=COVS, covariate_windows=WINDOWS, fit_window=FIT_WINDOW, seed=0)

# One panel, several specifications -> compare_estimators does the rest
cmp = compare_estimators({
    "VanillaSC (outcome-only)":    VanillaSC({**base}),
    "VanillaSC (malo, ADH cov)":   VanillaSC({**base, "backend": "malo",  **cov}),
    "VanillaSC (mscmt, ADH cov)":  VanillaSC({**base, "backend": "mscmt", **cov}),
    "CLUSTERSC (RPCA-SC)":         CLUSTERSC({**base, "method": "rpca", "rpca_method": "PCP"}),
    "CLUSTERSC (PCR/RSC)":         CLUSTERSC({**base, "method": "PCR"}),
}, show_bands=False)

# uniform-weight diagnostic (what a naive equal-weighted "SC" would fit)
wide = d.pivot(index="year", columns="country", values="gdp"); pre = wide.index < 1990
order = [c for c in wide.columns if c != "West Germany"]; X = wide[order].to_numpy()
obs_pre = wide["West Germany"].to_numpy()[pre]
uniform_rmse = float(np.sqrt(np.mean((obs_pre - X[pre] @ np.full(len(order), 1/len(order)))**2)))
```

```{python}
#| label: load-basc
basc_cf = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_counterfactual.csv"))
basc_w = pd.read_csv(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_weights.csv"))
basc_rmse = float(open(os.path.join(DATA, "basc_insample_rmse.txt")).read().strip())
n_basc = int((basc_w["weight"] > 0.01).sum())
```

## In-sample (pre-1990) RMSE by method

```{python}
#| label: tbl-rmse
tbl = (cmp.summary.rename(columns={"pre_rmse": "In-sample RMSE", "att": "ATT"})
       .reset_index().rename(columns={"method": "Method"}))
tbl = tbl[["Method", "In-sample RMSE", "ATT"]]
basc_rows = pd.DataFrame([
    {"Method": "BASC (2000 / 2000 MCMC)", "In-sample RMSE": round(basc_rmse, 1), "ATT": np.nan},
    {"Method": "BASC (500 / 500 MCMC)",   "In-sample RMSE": 186.2,               "ATT": np.nan},
])
full = pd.concat([tbl, basc_rows], ignore_index=True).sort_values("In-sample RMSE").reset_index(drop=True)
full.round(1)
```

Lower is better. Every standard-toolkit estimator — including the *robust*
`CLUSTERSC` methods, which deliberately denoise rather than chase in-sample fit —
attains a materially tighter pre-treatment fit than BASC. BASC's pre-1990 RMSE
(~169) is roughly 2.8x the plain `VanillaSC` fit (60.8) and ~1.7–1.9x the
`CLUSTERSC` fit (88.6 / 98.0). Giving BASC 4x the MCMC iterations (500 → 2000)
moved its RMSE only from 186 to ~169, so this is not a burn-in artifact. For
reference, *uniform* (1/16) donor weights fit ~21x worse (RMSE ~1289).

## Observed vs synthetic West Germany

```{python}
#| label: fig-paths
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
cmp.plot(ax=ax)                                   # mlsynth methods + observed
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["synthetic"], color="#d62728", lw=2.2,
        label="BASC", zorder=5)
ax.axvspan(basc_cf["year"].min(), 1990, color="0.92", zorder=0)
ax.axvline(1990, color="grey", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_title("West Germany: observed vs synthetic counterfactuals")
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left"); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

The gap of interest is inside the shaded pre-1990 window: the standard
estimators track observed West Germany closely, while BASC departs from it
*before* treatment — the signature of a worse synthetic control.

## BASC counterfactual with its 95% credible band

```{python}
#| label: fig-basc-ci
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.axvspan(basc_cf["year"].min(), 1990, color="0.92", zorder=0)
ax.fill_between(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["lo95"], basc_cf["hi95"], color="#d62728", alpha=0.15, label="BASC 95% CI")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["synthetic"], color="#d62728", lw=2, label="BASC synthetic")
ax.plot(basc_cf["year"], basc_cf["observed"], color="black", lw=2.4, label="West Germany (observed)")
ax.axvline(1990, color="grey", ls=":", lw=1)
ax.set_xlabel("year"); ax.set_ylabel("GDP per capita")
ax.set_title("BASC posterior counterfactual (2000/2000)")
ax.legend(fontsize=8, loc="upper left"); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## Donor weights: standard SCM is sparse and Austria-led

```{python}
#| label: fig-weights
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4.2))
cmp.plot_weights(ax=ax)                           # cmp.weights, now populated
ax.set_title("Donor weights by standard-SCM specification")
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-weights
wide_w = cmp.weights.pivot(index="donor", columns="method", values="weight").fillna(0.0)
wide_w["ADH 2015 (published)"] = pd.Series(
    {"Austria": 0.42, "USA": 0.22, "Japan": 0.16, "Switzerland": 0.11, "Netherlands": 0.09})
wide_w["BASC (2000/2000)"] = basc_w.set_index("donor")["weight"]
wide_w = wide_w.fillna(0.0).round(3)
wide_w = wide_w.loc[(wide_w > 0.01).any(axis=1)]
o = ["Austria", "USA", "Japan", "Switzerland", "Netherlands", "Italy", "Greece", "France", "Norway"]
wide_w.reindex([x for x in o if x in wide_w.index] + [x for x in wide_w.index if x not in o])
```

Every standard-SCM path — outcome-only, the `malo` global optimum, the `mscmt`
nested-DE search with the exact ADH covariate spec, and the published ADH 2015
weights — concentrates on a handful of donors led by Austria. A *uniform* vector
is unreachable by the Abadie quadratic program and fits ~21x worse, so it cannot
be the output of "standard SCM." BASC selects a sparse set (Switzerland / Japan /
Italy) but assigns near-zero weight to **Austria and the USA** — the two donors
every standard analysis identifies as most important — which is why its
pre-treatment fit is worse.

## Bottom line

A method that claims synthetic-control superiority should at least match the
standard toolkit's pre-treatment fit. On the authors' own West Germany data,
BASC does not: its in-sample RMSE is ~2–3x that of `VanillaSC` and worse than the
robust `CLUSTERSC` estimators, and it excludes the canonical Austria/USA donors.
BASC is Bayesian, so some in-sample-fit sacrifice from its donor-selection and GP
priors is expected — but the paper presents it as a synthetic control and claims
competitiveness, its GP flexibility term is inactive here (mean |f| ≈ 0.4 over
the pre-period), and no direct in-sample-fit comparison against the standard
toolkit is provided.

---

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
`mlsynth` analyses (`VanillaSC`, `CLUSTERSC`, assembled with `compare_estimators`)
run at render time on `basedata/repgermany.dta`. BASC results are precomputed by
`scripts/basc_run.R` on the authors' sampler (`github.com/sll-lee/paper-BASC`),
original per-capita GDP scale. Panel: Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller (2015),
*Comparative Politics and the Synthetic Control Method*.
:::
